coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots derived from waste acorn cups and its application as an ultraviolet absorbent for polyvinyl alcohol film
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Nan
Gao, Shiyu
Xu, Changyan
Fang, Yanming
Xu, Li
Zhang, Wenqing
coredata.dc:description: Extracting effective ultraviolet (UV) absorbent from biomass-based material is urgent for the sustainable development. Here, non-doped CQDs (1-CQDs) and nitrogen-doped CQDs (2-CQDs) were synthesized from acorn cups (ACs) with a green method. The active surface groups of 1-CQDs (4.16 nm) include Ph-OH, OH, and COOH, etc., while for 2-CQDs (5.23 nm), nitrogen doping provides a large number of nitrogen atoms, so that the active functional groups include CNH2, CNC, CNH and CONH, etc. The carbon network of 1-CQDs is dominant for blue emission, the subordinate blue-green emission is derived from ellagitannin. For 2-CQDs, green emission comes from the flavonoid produced during hydrothermal carbonization and nitrogen doping processes. We combined chitosan (CS) into CQDs to synergistically act as UV absorbents for functionalizing PVA films. The UV-A blocking capacity and transparency of the PVA/CS/1-CQDs film are 83.58 % and 79.34 %, respectively. The PVA/CS/2-CQDs film can block UV-A up to 94.53 %. This study provides a new way for the clean and high-valued-added utilization of waste ACs, discusses the process and mechanism of extracting CQDs from ACs, and applies it to UV light conversion system of PVA film. It presents a reference for the development of a new generation of UV absorbents.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photographs under visible light (left) and the corresponding photograph under an UV lamp (365 nm) (right) of 1-CQDs (a) and 2-CQDs (b).
The schematic route of CQDs from ACs and its application as an UV absorbent for PVA film.

TEM (left) and HRTEM (right) images of 1-CQDs (a) and 2-CQDs (b). Size distribution histograms of 1-CQDs (c) and 2-CQDs (d) measured from TEM image based on 125 randomly selected nanoparticles, respectively.

FT-IR spectra (a) of ACs powder, 1-CQDs, and 2-CQDs. XPS full-scan spectra (b). The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s (c), N 1s (d), and O 1s (e) of 1-CQD (upper) and 2-CQDs (bottom). The structure (f) of 1-CQDs and 2-CQDs.
Schematic diagram of the conversion process from ACs to 1-CQDs (a), and the nitrogen-doping process from ACs to 2-CQDs (b).

PL emission spectra of 1-CQDs (upper) and 2-CQDs (bottom) excited at different wavelengths (left), and the corresponding emission behavior at the optimum excitation wavelength (right) (a), the corresponding color coordinate (b), and the ESTPT reaction process of the molecule under light excited (c).
UV–Vis absorption spectra of the prepared CQDs (a), the fluorescence lifetime of 1-CQDs at 360 nm (b) and 400 nm (c), and the fluorescence lifetime of 2-CQDs at 420 nm (d).

Effect of pH (a) and diluted factor (b) on PL emission behavior of 1-CQDs (0.1 g/mL, upper) and 2-CQDs (0.1 g/mL, bottom), and the corresponding tendency excited at the optimum excitation wavelength (c).
UV–Vis transmittance of the PVA/CS/CQDs films with different content of 1-CQDs (a), 2-CQDs (b), 1CS (c), 2CS (d), and the intuitive comparison of UV–vis performance of 1CQDs-12 (e) and 2CQDs-10 (f).

Photograph of the 1CQDs-12 film (a) and 2CQDs-10 film (b) under visible light (left) and excited by 365 nm (right), as well as the corresponding UV–vis absorption spectra (c). The PL emission spectra of the 1CQDs-10 film (d) and 2CQDs-10 film (e) are excited under different wavelengths.
The FT-IR of the prepared components films (a), the possible hydrogen bond formation (left), and SEM micrograph of the cross-section (right) of 1CQDs-12 (b), 2CQDs-10 (c).
Comparison of UV-blocking properties of our PVA-based films with those of commercially available UV-blocking films and UV-blocking films reported in other papers.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Extracting effective ultraviolet (UV) absorbent from biomass-based material is urgent for the sustainable development. Here, non-doped CQDs (1-CQDs) and nitrogen-doped CQDs (2-CQDs) were synthesized from acorn cups (ACs) with a green method. The active surface groups of 1-CQDs (4.16 nm) include Ph-OH, OH, and COOH, etc., while for 2-CQDs (5.23 nm), nitrogen doping provides a large number of nitrogen atoms, so that the active functional groups include CNH2, CNC, CNH and CONH, etc. The carbon network of 1-CQDs is dominant for blue emission, the subordinate blue-green emission is derived from ellagitannin. For 2-CQDs, green emission comes from the flavonoid produced during hydrothermal carbonization and nitrogen doping processes. We combined chitosan (CS) into CQDs to synergistically act as UV absorbents for functionalizing PVA films. The UV-A blocking capacity and transparency of the PVA/CS/1-CQDs film are 83.58 % and 79.34 %, respectively. The PVA/CS/2-CQDs film can block UV-A up to 94.53 %. This study provides a new way for the clean and high-valued-added utilization of waste ACs, discusses the process and mechanism of extracting CQDs from ACs, and applies it to UV light conversion system of PVA film. It presents a reference for the development of a new generation of UV absorbents.

ce:section.ce:para: Ultraviolet (UV) radiation that reaches the Earth's surface is mainly UV-A (315–400 nm), with a small amount of UV-B (280–315 nm) [1]. Organic systems are susceptible to UV radiation because they would accelerate photodegradation reactions during long-term outdoor exposure, such as dyes fading, polymeric materials photoaging, and skin aging [2]. In practical application, adding UV absorbents into photosensitive material is the most effective and convenient method to protect them. Inorganic UV absorbent is considered a traditional and effective additive to prevent UV radiation. It is generally dispersed in the substrate and works by absorbing, reflecting, and scattering UV light [3]. However, its use remains limited, as most inorganic UV absorbents are suitable only as white pigments in white or opaque products [3]. In addition, there are health and environment issues in use due to its toxicity and potential bioaccumulation [4,5]. The most commercial organic UV absorbents, such as benzophenone, benzotriazole and other o-hydroxy aromatic compounds [6], always contain ample conjugated structures. The photostability mechanism of these types of compounds involves the excited state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT) photophysical process by O…H…O and O…H…N tunnels [7]. The rapid ESIPT reaction makes it efficient to transform the absorbed radiation energy into harmless heat, fluorescence, or phosphorescence [8,9]. However, it has been reported that organic UV absorbents are unstable and prone to biodegradable, and excessive exposure to UV radiation will produce toxic photo-adducts [10]. It is worth mentioning that an ideal UV absorbent used in photosensitive material would be expected to absorb all UV light but not visible light, and keep high photostability [11]. So, the trade-off between transparency in the visual and UV-blocking capacity also should be taken into account. Therefore, it is urgent to prepare a biodegradable and nontoxic UV absorbent with excellent UV-blocking property and transparency performance in visible light.

In this regard, carbon dots (CDs) has great potential as a novel UV absorbent because it can emit visible light under UV excitation [12,13]. CDs is widely considered as a discrete quasi-spherical nanoparticle with particle size less than 10 nm [14], which has size-confinement effect and photoluminescent (PL) properties [15]. In the CDs family, biomass carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from renewable materials has received significant attention in recent years due to its sustainability, biocompatibility, and large-scale availability at a low cost. Many biomass residues have been successfully used to synthesize CQDs such as walnut shells [16] or date palm fronds [17] by carbonization pyrolysis [18], coffee grounds by hydrothermal treatment [19,20], bombyx mori silk by solvothermal treatment [21], sugarcane bagasse pulp by combustion treatment [22], lotus root by microwave treatment [23], etc.……. Furthermore, a large number of studies have shown that introducing heteroatoms or solvent into CQDs, such as N, P, S, B, and hydrogen peroxide can significantly improve the optical property of CQDs [24–27]. However, the mechanism of heteroatom doping to enhance the fluorescence performance of pure CQDs is still controversial and needs to be further explored. Specially, due to the complex composition of biomass carbon source after hydrothermal treatment, most previous reports did not discuss the contribution of the component of the original carbon source to the formation of the final CQDs. We believe that the systematic exploration of the contribution of the chemical components in the carbon source to the fluorescence properties of the resulting CQDs will be helpful for the selection and customization of raw materials for the preparation of CQDs in the future study. In addition, most reports on the application of CQDs always focus on the field of cell bioimaging, or detection of harmful metal ions and other small molecule. It is a very beneficial attempt to apply biomass CQDs as degradable UV absorbents for functionalization photodegradable films. As far as available published literature is concerned, the acorn cups (ACs) has not been found in biomass CQDs' carbon source families. Quercus variabilis (Fagaceae) is a woody ecological genus distributed in the Northern Hemisphere [28]. ACs is the main by-product of Quercus variabilis, which is rich in tannins and phenolic compounds [29]. It is widely used in the extraction of ellagitannin [30], a commodity with low value-added [31]. More importantly, the extraction process of ellagitannin from ACs results in serious water pollution, which runs counter to the current increasingly stringent environmental protection requirements. Considering the low value-added quality of ellagitannin and the high secondary pollution of extracting process, this study explored the possibility of ACs as a carbon source for synthesizing CQDs. Among the synthetic method, hydrothermal carbonization is considered a scalable and flexibility conversion technology to synthesize biomass CQDs. In addition, the flavonoid compound is a point that cannot be ignored during the hydrothermal treatment process of ACs. It is a secondary metabolite of ACs, and is thought to be prospective in inflammatory and oxidative due to its rich antioxidant capacity [29,32].

The purpose of this study was to prepare biomass CQDs and nitrogen-doped CQDs from waste ACs by hydrothermal carbonization treatment, and to explore the synthesis mechanism and potential applications as UV absorbent in the polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) composite film. In addition, we found the CQDs extracted from ACs had good adaptability in acidic aqueous solution. Chitosan (CS) not only has a certain ability to block UV light [33,34], but also is a good film-forming substrate (containing amino and hydroxyl groups). Meanwhile, it also has the advantages of non-toxicity, biodegradability and good biocompatibility [35]. Therefore, CS solution was used to adjust the pH value of CQDs solution to acidity, so as to explore the synergistic UV-blocking performance of CS and CQDs. The CQDs enhanced by the CS as a UV absorbent are then blended into PVA by casting. The UV-blocking property of functioned PVA films are further evaluated.

Acorn cups used in the experiment were collected from Nanjing Forestry University, China. Both acetic acid (CH3COOH, ≥ 99.5%) and ethylenediamine (EDA) came from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Nanjing, China). Chitosan (degree of deacetylation, ≥95%; viscosity, 100–200 m. Pas) and phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) with different pH values were supplied by Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and Haibiao Technologies Co., Ltd. (Fuzhou, China), respectively. Quinine sulfate (99.0%), Na2CO3, Folin-Ciocalteu reagent (1 mol/L), gallic acid and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) (Molecular weight 14,5000 g mol−1, 99.0–99.4 mol% hydrolyzed) were procured from Aladdin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents were used as received without further purification. Deionized water with a resistivity of 18.25 MΩ was used.

The collected ACs were washed with water to remove all attachments and left in sunlight until the moisture content was less than 15%. And then crushed using an omnipotent disintegrator (400 Y, Yongkang Boao Hardware Products Co. Ltd., Zhejiang, China), and finally passed through a sieve with 90 mesh. Referring to the research of Irmania [36], CQDs derived from ACs was synthesized via a hydrothermal method. Briefly, the initial reaction mixture of the sieved ACs powder (0.3 g) and deionized water (30.3 mL) was transferred into a Teflon reactor with stainless steel autoclave (50 mL, Taizhou, China) and kept in compressed water at 200 °C for 8 h. After being cooled down to room temperature, the resultant solution was removed from the reactor and purified with a piece of filtration membrane with 0.22 µm pores to remove residues and then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. The obtained yellowish-brown transparent supernatant (as shown in Fig. 1 a) was labeled as 1-CQDs. Also, the dark brown supernatant (as shown in Fig. 1b) labeled as 2-CQDs was synthesized in the same way as that of the sample 1-CQDs, except that the formula of the initial reaction mixture consisted of ACs powder (0.3g), deionized water (30 mL) and EDA (300 μL). The particle concentration of 1-CQDs and 2-CQDs measured by an absorption spectrum method [37] was 4 mg/mL and 10 mg/mL, respectively. The prepared CQDs solution was stored in brown bottles at 4°C for use. CQDs powder was obtained by freeze-drying the CQDs solution (Alpha 1–2 LD plus, Germany) at −65 °C under vacuum for 3 d.

The solution casting technique [38] was used to prepare PVA/CS/CQDs films. PVA solution (10 wt%) was obtained by dissolving PVA granules (10 g) in deionized water (90 g) with continuous agitation at 95°C. CS aqueous solution (2 wt%), which was used to keep the pH value of the reaction mixture at 4.5, was prepared by dissolving CS (2 g) into acetic acid (0.1 M, 98 g) under continuous magneticstirring at room temperature. The CQDs solution (0.1 g/mL) was obtained by diluting the as-prepared CQDs solution with deionized water. A certain amount of PVA solution (10 wt%), CS solution (2 wt%), and CQDs solution (0.1 g/mL) according to the formula in Table 1 were mixed with the aid of ultrasonic agitation, then poured into a glass mold and dried in a vacuum oven at 50°C until the films could be detached from the mold, resulting in a piece of PVA/CS/CQDs composite film. The prepared film was kept in a drying vessel to maintain its moisture content below 5 % for characteristics. Fig. 2 shows the schematic route of CQDs from ACs and the corresponding PVA/CS/CQDs composite films.

The pH value of the as-prepared CQDs solution was determined using a digital pH meter (pHS-3C, INESA Scientific Instrument, China). The pictures of high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) of the CQDs solution was obtained on a JEM-2100 UHR (JEOL, Japan) microscope operated at 200 kV using ultrathin carbon film-coated copper grids. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the raw material (the sieved ACs powder) and the prepared CQDs powder were acquired on a VERTEX 80 V spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) using the KBr pellet technique. The spectra scanning range and the resolutions were 4000–500 cm−1 and 4 cm−1, respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement of the CQDs powder was conducted on an AXIS UltraDLD electron spectrometer (Shimadzu, UK), and the binding energy was calibrated based on the C 1s peak at 284.6 eV. Photoluminescent (PL) emission spectra of diluted CQDs solution were recorded by a LS 55 instrument (PerkinElmer, USA), and the slit width of excitation and emission were set as 5 nm and 10 nm, respectively. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra of diluted CQDs solution were measured using a Lambda 950 UV–Visible spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). Photoluminescent quantum yield (PLQY) is the number of emitted photons relative to the number of absorbed photons. The PLQY of the synthesized CQDs was tested according to an established relative method [39]. Here, quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 was chosen as the reference (PLQY = 54 %, excited at 360 nm), and the relative PLQY of the CQDs was calculated using equation (1),

Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of CQDs samples were measured under the optimized excitation wavelength using a FLS 980 time-correlated single-photon counting system (EI, UK). The obtained lifetime value was fitted well via a double-exponential function (Equation (2)), and the average lifetime was calculated according to equation (3) [40],

Total phenolics content in the CQDs was determined by a modified colorimetric method [41] using Folin-Ciocalteu (FC) reagent and expressed with gallic acid equivalent (GAE). Firstly, the absorbance of gallic acid with concentrations ranging from 0.02 mg/mL to 0.4 mg/mL was measured at 765 nm, and then an absorption standard calibration curve was fitted from this absorption of gallic acid and its concentration (y = 0.2213x − 0.0013, R2 = 0.9995). Secondly, diluted CQD aqueous solution (1 mL, 0.2 mg/mL), Na2CO3 (10 mL, 10 wt%), FC (2 mL) and distilled water were added into a volumetric flask (25 mL) and left at 50°C for 1 h in the dark condition. The specific absorbance of the mixed solution at 765 nm was immediately measured. Total phenolic of the CQDs was expressed as mg GAE/g using the equation obtained from a calibration curve of gallic acid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of the prepared PVA/CS/CQDs films
ce:section.ce:para:
The thickness of the PVA/CS/CQDs film was measured using a thickness meter. The measuring was carried out at 5 random locations on the film surface and the average value of 5 test data was reported. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the films, which were fixed on slides with adhesive carbon tapes and metalized with Au, were observed and photographed using a JSE-7600 (JSOL, Japan) instrument at an accelerating voltage of 5 kV. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the films were measured using (Bomen 102. ABB Ltd, Zurich Switzerland) with a Universal Attenuated Total Reflectance (UATR) accessory with the diamond crystal. This analysis was done at wavenumber ranged from 400 to 4000 cm−1 and with a scan frequency of 32 times per second. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) of the relevant CS and PVA composite films were collected on an Ultima IV Goniometer equipped with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å), with the targeted voltage of 40 kV and current of 30 mA. The zeta potential of CQDs solutions and CS/CQDs dispersions were tested using a NanoPlus 2 zeta analyzer (Otsuka electronics Co., Ltd, Japan). The measurement of zeta potentials for each sample was performed 3 times at 25 °C, and the average values were used. UV–vis absorbance and transmittance (200–800 nm) of the film measured using a Lambda 950 UV–Visible spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). Photoluminescent (PL) emission spectra of the PVA/CS/CQDs films were recorded by a LS 55 instrument (PerkinElmer, USA), and the slit width of excitation and emission were set as 5 nm and 10 nm, respectively. The UV-blocking property of the films is intuitively depicted by the average transmittance of UV-A and UV-B, which are calculated using equation (4) and (5) [42], respectively. In addition, the visual appearance of the films through UV–vis transmittance spectra is calculated using equation (6). Besides, the UV-blocking factor is also used to evaluate the UV protection property [42,43], it is calculated using equation (7)

TUV-A , TUV-B and TVisible are average transmittance in UV-A (315–400 nm), UV-B (280–315 nm) and visible light (400–760 nm) region, respectively. The photobleaching property of the films was tested by measuring the UV–vis transmission after exposing to UV light (365 nm) for 6, 12, 24 h using a ZF-1 Ultraviolet Analyzer (Lichen, China).

Fig. 3 shows the morphology, particle size and distribution of the obtained 1-CQDs and 2-CQDs. There are a lot of nanoparticles with spherical morphology in the two CQDs samples. These nanoparticles are evenly distributed in solution without multiple nuclei embedded, suggesting that the presence of organic groups on a single carbon sphere surface [12]. Compared to the 1-CQDs sample, the 2-CQDs sample displays homogeneous nucleation with a looser carbon sphere structure, resulting in a significant increase in particle size. Uniform morphology and deeper carbonization of 2-CQDs is due to carbon chains' growth into nuclei by continuous polymerization. EDA, most possibly as an alkali dopant, effectively facilitates the hydrothermal carbonization of ACs. Moreover, both samples show high crystallinity with an interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100 facets) lattice plane of graphitic carbon [44]. It demonstrates that the carbon core of CQDs possesses graphite-like sp2-hybridized carbon nanodomains. And the formation of the crystalline structure is possibly caused by the intensive temperature and high steam pressure in the synthesis reaction. Size distribution histograms of 1-CQDs and 2-CQDs obtained from TEM images are also presented in Fig. 3(c-d). Most particles of 1-CQDs are distributed in the range of 2–6 nm with an average diameter of 4.16 nm, whereas most particle sizes range of 2-CQDs are 4–7 nm and the average diameter is 5.23 nm. And, of course, the larger particle diameter, the more sites are provided for varying fluorophore molecules, and that is the advantage of nitrogen-doping.

The chemical component and structure of the as-synthesized CQDs are further explored by FT-IR and XPS. Fig. 4 a shows FT-IR spectra of the ACs powder used in the experiment and the corresponding CQDs. The spectra of the 1-CQDs and the starting material (ACs powder) are almost the same, indicating that there are not new functional groups generate, only take place hydrolytic reaction, condensation, or polymerization reaction during the synthesis of 1-CQDs. However, the 2-CQDs presents a different spectrum from those of 1-CQDs and ACs powder. Although all spectra present an absorption peak at 3438 cm−1 (attributed to OH stretching vibration), this peak for 2-CQDs is the sharpest. It is attributed to the vibration of free amino groups on the surface of 2-CQDs overlapped with OH. The characteristic absorption peaks at 1030 cm−1 and 1776 cm−1 are assigned to stretching vibration of COC and CO in the inner fat, respectively. The peaks at 2852 cm−1 and 2922 cm−1 are related to stretching vibration of CH2 and CH3, respectively. The small absorption peak at 1315 cm−1 is assigned to the bending vibration of CH. Moreover, the sharp peak at 1620 cm−1 is ascribed to CO stretching vibration of amide [45]. Therefore, the formation of the amide is by consuming the COOH groups and NH2 groups in the nitrogen-doped process. The absorption peaks at 1100 cm−1 and 1224 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibration of COH and phenolic hydroxyl (Ph-OH). The existence of Ph-OH indicates that aromatic compounds exist in the ACs powder and 1-CQDs, whereas the disappearance of Ph-OH for 2-CQDs suggests the Ph-OH groups are consumed. The nucleophilic reaction occurred in 2-CQDs, this is, the amine group replaces the Ph-OH group. Two new shoulder peaks of 2-CQDs at 916 cm−1 and 1196 cm−1 are assigned to bending vibration of N-H and stretching vibration of CNH2, respectively. In addition, the spectrum of 2-CQDs presents a more intense stretching vibration of CNC at 1400 cm−1 than that of 1-CQDs, which indicates the NH2 groups successfully doped into carbon chains. According to the above discussion, 1-CQDs possesses many reactive groups such as Ph-OH, OH, and COOH, which can be modified by NH2 groups, and functional groups CNH2, CNC and CONH coexisted on the 2-CQD surface.

Fig. 4(b-e) lists XPS full-scan spectra and the corresponding high-resolution spectrum of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s of the prepared 1-CQDs and 2-CQDs. Compared with 1-CQDs, 2-CQDs has a higher N content (12.14% to 3.43%), a lower O content (16.33% to 28.18%) and a similar C content (71.53 to 68.39%). The O content of 2-CQDs decreases with the increase of N atomic percentage, indicating partial reconstruction of graphene structures during the nitrogen-doping process. It confirms that N atoms have been doped into the structure of 2-CQDs, as mentioned in the discussion of FT-IR spectra. Fig. 4c shows that both C 1s spectra of 1-CQDs and 2-CQDs show one main peak at 284.5–284.8 eV, which corresponds to sp2-hybridized graphitic C that most C atoms exist in the form of conjugated honeycomb lattice [46]. In addition, the high-resolution C 1s spectra can be deconvoluted into three Gaussian peaks center. And two peaks center at 283.6 eV and 284.9 eV for both spectra, which are attributed to CH and CC/CC, respectively; a small peak for 1-CQDs at 286 eV and another peak for 2-CQDs at 286.6 eV correspond to the bonding formation of N atoms related to sp2-C and sp3-C [47], respectively, which is attributed to CO/CN [48,49]. In the N 1s spectra illustrated in Fig. 4d, there exists a peak with lower binding energy at 398.4 eV for both spectra, indicating the existence of CNH (pyridine-like) in both 1-CQDs and 2-CQDs; and another peak at 400.2 eV for 1-CQDs and 399.3 eV for 2-CQDs, which is ascribed to pyrrolic N and NH2, respectively. Therefore, it can be deduced that 2-CQDs contains a small amount of pyrrolic N overlapped with abundant NH2 groups. The formation of pyrrolic N due to the dehydration reaction between the neighbor COOH and NH2 groups [50]. In addition, from the spectra of deconvoluted Gaussian peaks, it also can be found that in Fig. 4d, CNH group is the main N component for 1-CQDs, which is related to the compounds of ACs. Therefore, nitrogen-containing functional groups such as CNH, CNOH, CNC and NH2 exist on the surface of 2-CQDs. Notably, 1-CQDs and 2-CQDs have different O 1 s spectra, as shown in Fig. 4e. The O 1 s spectrum of 1-CQDs is converted into three Gaussian peaks centered at 529.7 eV, 531 eV, and 532.6 eV, which are attributed to COH, CO, and CO, respectively. However, the O 1 s spectra of 2-CQDs presents two peaks at 530.5 eV and 532.8 eV, which is assigned to the signals of CO and NCO, respectively.

The results of XPS and FT-IR suggest that the obtained CQDs is easily functionalized. Nitrogen-doped CQDs has a more complex hybridization in the compositions and chemical structure compared with non-doped CQDs. Experimentally, the nucleophilic substitution and dehydration reaction between amine moieties with oxygen-functional groups play a crucial role in producing nitrogen-functionalized CQDs. There exist plentiful oxygen-containing groups on the surface of CQDs. Therefore, they display good water-dispersibility, which makes its application advantageous in aqueous media. As discussed above, the two types of CQDs are mainly composed of carbon core itself containing sp2-hybridized carbon nanodomains, and their surface is occupied by a large number of different functional groups, as graphically shown in Fig. 4f.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preliminary study on the formation mechanism of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The main components of ACs are lignocellulose and organic molecules such as carbohydrate, lignin, ellagitannin, and flavonoid compounds. Carbohydrate, including cellulose and hemicellulose, is the most abundant organic compound in nature and is tightly bound to lignin in plant walls. The ellagitannin contains sufficient phenolic hydroxyl (the o-phenolic hydroxyl groups is typical) and carboxyl groups [32,51]. Flavonoid compounds naturally exist in the cell wall or cytoplasm of ACs [52,53] and are easy to be recognized due to their low molecular content and noticeable colors [54]. Based on the above chemical composition of ACs and our experimental results, we explored the growth mechanism of 1-CQDs, as shown in Fig. 5 a. High-temperature water as a medium provides a kinetic decomposition phase for the heterogeneous system [55] and facilitates the complex pyrolysis reaction of ACs. Hemicellulose is mainly decomposed into soluble oligomers and monosaccharides during the hydrothermal process. These organic molecule clusters are formed through intermolecular and intramolecular dehydration [56]. For cellulose, it is hydrolyzed to oligomers at the initial stage of hydrolysis, then dehydrated to soluble products, and then soluble polymers are formed by condensation or polymerization reactions [57,58]. With the increase of reaction time, the final carbon structure changes from a carbonaceous polyfuran to an increasingly expanding carbon network [59]. Simultaneously, organic polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) such as lignin gradually become fragments, which are finally polymerized through molecules self-assemble into a carbon network with specific groups [60]. In short, tissues of ACs are broken into small molecules during the hydrothermal process, which are further combined into PAHs clusters through self-assembly to form the carbon core of CQDs and finally form the fluorescent nanostructure. Therefore, the synthesis process of biomass CQDs is more than likely to contain functionalized PAHs of varying sizes. It should be mentioned that ellagitannin is also released during the hydrothermal cracking process of ACs, which is easier to aromatized and carbonized and become PAHs clusters during further heating [61,62]. Besides, other reaction routes may coexist in the hydrothermal process. Organic acids such as glycolic acid, formic acid, acetic acid, and lactic acid also are released in the hydrothermal stage [63]. The hydrothermal carbonization process of ACs presents enormous complexity in structure and distribution on superficial areas of the carbon sphere by self-assembly of these fragment units.

The pH values of 1-CQDs and 2-CQDs are 4.04 and 10.60, respectively. The 1-CQDs solution in a weak acid state is because of it contains plentiful polyphenolic hydroxyl groups and organic acids, whereas the alkaline acceptor for 2-CQDs is caused by the nitrogen atoms in the heterocyclic compounds or the oxygen atoms in the carbonyl group [2]. In addition,zeta potential of as-prepared CQDs solution are negatively charged (1-CQDs is −28.53 mV and 2-CQDs is −10.57 mV), which confirmed the presence of carboxyl andhydroxylfunctional groups on the surface. According to the analysis results of FTIR and XPS of the prepared CQDs, we believe that the amine groups in EDA virtually combine with various functional groups, there is an intramolecular dehydration reaction with neighbor COOH groups, finally form the amide groups. At the same time, a part of the amine group replaces the Ph-OH groups, part of the amine group can be incorporated into carbon ring's structure, and part of the amine group is in the free motion state.

According to the colorimetric method described in 2.2.3, the total phenol content is of 1-CQDs and 2-CQDs is 0.58 mg GAE/mL and 1.325 mg GAE/mL, respectively. It indicates that even some Ph-OH groups are consumed during the synthetic process of 2-CQDs, its total phenol level is still much higher than that of 1-CQDs. They are combined with the experiment result described in 2.2.1 (the presence of flavonoids in 2-CQDs makes it darker in color than 1-CQDs). Therefore, the introduced EDA in the synthesis of 2-CQDs provides an alkaline condition for the hydrothermal carbonization process, which is more conducive to releasing flavonoid compounds in ACs. Under alkaline conditions, the hydrothermal reaction of the above macromolecules of ACs is more intense. The reaction of splitting fragments into PAHs clusters through self-assembly and further forming carbon nuclei fluorescent structures, the fluorophores are attached to the carbon core and finally form 2-CQDs. Thus, EDA plays a crucial role in the formation process of 2-CQDs, and the nitrogen-doping process of 2-CQD is shown in Fig. 5b.

Above the results, the formation of CQDs could be attributed to the carbonization of carbohydrates, self-assembly of PAHs, and organic synthesis from small molecules. Note that the intense reactions could promote the nucleation, growth of carbon substances and simultaneously introduce multiple functional groups on the surface of resultant nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the prepared CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: PL emission behavior is a characteristic optical performance of CQDs, which directly affects its application scope. In addition, the causal relationship between the structure of CQDs and its emission characteristics is worth exploring. As shown in Fig. 1, when exposed to UV light (365 nm), the 1-CQDs emits bright blue light, whereas the 2-CQDs emits bright blue-green light, which suggests the nitrogen-doped process changes the original emission behavior. It is worth noting that the as-synthesized CQDs can emit visible light when excited by UV light, which provides a visual theoretical basis as a UV transformation medium.

In Fig. 6 a, both 1-CQDs and 2-CQDs exhibit broad emission spectra (ranging from the UV light to the near-infrared light) with narrowed emission peak widths when excited by light with a wavelength of 300–430 nm, and 350–480 nm for 1-CQDs and 2-CQDs, respectively. It indicates that the tunable PL emission of the CQDs can be realized by adjusting the excitation wavelength. In addition, the PL emission spectra of both CQDs shown in Fig. 6a are different. 1-CQDs displays an excitation-independent behavior when excited light with a wavelength of 300–370 nm and 390–430 nm. However, 2-CQDs presents a typical excitation-dependent behavior, that is, the emission peak of the PL spectrum is redshifted with the increase of excitation wavelength and displays a rough symmetry on the X-axis. Besides, the response characteristics of 1-CQDs and 2-CQDs on UV–vis light excitation are different. When excited by 360 nm UV light, 1-CQDs has the strongest blue emission peak at 436 nm and a weaker blue-green emission peak around at 495 nm. For 2-CQDs, the maximum emission peak is located at 534 nm when excited by 420 nm light. Although both 1-CQDs and 2-CQDs show panchromatic emission at the optimal excitation wavelength, the former possesses a typical blue emission behavior, while the latter shows strong green emission characteristics. Studies have shown that the PL mechanism of CQDs is related to the radiative recombination between electrons and holes, which occurs on the superficial sites of CQDs and is trapped by light [64–66]. This means that hybridization of the carbon core and connected groups plays a leading role in the PL behavior of the CQDs. The two independent emission peaks of 1-CQDs indicate that it has two radiative recombination centers [67], the carbon cores are dominant for PL emission and the PAHs generated from ellagitannin with the homogeneous chemical structure are subordinate for that.

And the particle size is also considered to cause the change energy band gap, which affects the final optical properties of the CQDs. For 1-CQDs, dominated by the carbon cores, it is considered that the particle size is the main reason for the change of energy band gap, which affects the final optical properties of the CQDs [68]. For 2-CQDs, the nitrogen-doping process increases photo-excited electrons in the emission transition channel, enhancing the radiation transition probability and the PL intensity [69], and lowering the band gap of CQDs or forming inter-band states [70]. It suggests that there are different contributions to the PL emission due to the different emission traps sites distribution [71], originating from carbon cores and PAHs domains from varying fluorophore molecules compounded with the abundance of functional groups. It can be seen from Fig. 6b that the chromaticity coordinates of 1-CQDs and 2-CQDs at their respective optimal excitation wavelengths are [0.1735, 0.1598] and [0.2279, 0.1607], corresponding to the blue and green light, respectively. This result further confirms the primary PL emission characteristics of CQDs. We believe that the more fluorophore molecules are formed in nitrogen-doping, which contributes significantly to the green emission of 2-CQDs.

Combined with the specific structure and optical properties of the two CQDs prepared, it can be speculated that ESIPT reaction occurs in photoexcitation of O-H- type molecules widely present in phenolic groups (near heterocyclic base) and hydroxyl groups (at the 3-position of flavonoid compound) [72]. Besides, the N-H- type fluorophores also usually undergo ESIPT reactions at an ultrafast rate [73,74]. We speculate that the ESIPT reaction takes place in organic fluorophore molecules of CQDs by undergoing photoexcitation. This reaction starts from the normal ground-state (N) to the excited-state (N*), followed become an electronic excitation tautomeric form (T*) by translocation of the proton, then T* is connected to the ground-state tautomer (T) in the form of fluorescence emission. T undergoes a fast reverse of the proton transfer, finally returns to the normal ground-state, closing a reaction cycle [75,76], as shown in Fig. 6c. In addition, the PLQY of 1-CQDs and 2-CQDs measured according to the method in 2.3 is 2.24 % and 7.38 %, respectively, indicating that the addition of amino compounds during the synthesis of CQDs could significantly improve its PLQY.

Fig. 7 a compares the UV–vis absorption spectra of 1-CQDs and 2-CQDs. Both CQDs samples exhibit significant absorption in the wavelength range of 250–400 nm. 2-CQDs has a stronger absorption peak because of vast flavonoid compounds, making it need to be diluted more than 1-CQDs to prevent re-absorption. Allen NS. et al. believed that the light stability of flavonoid compounds could be attributed to the screening of harmful radiation (minimum penetration), the selective absorption of the incident light, and the deactivation of photoexcited species to some extent [77]. For both 1-CQDs and 2-CQDs, the absorption peak at around 276 nm is generally assigned to the π-π* transition of CC bond, originating from the conjugated π-orbitals of the carbon rings and related to the (crystalline) carbon core [78,79]. Therefore, the core state of CQDs is the main absorption characteristic. The weak absorption peak of 1-CQDs at around 340 nm belongs to the n-π* edge transition of the CO bond or other connected groups [80]. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of 2-CQDs displays a lot of small peaks within the wavelength of 300–370 nm, which indicates that the nitrogen-doping process increases the surface absorption state of the prepared CQDs, resulting in the green light emission of 2-CQDs.

To further analyze the PL emission source of CQDs, the time-resolved PL decay curve of the prepared CQDs is shown in Fig. 7(b-d), and the decay fitting results are listed in Table S1 (see ESI). Short-lived component τ1 and long-lived component τ2 are related to the electronic transition of CQDs [81], the τ1 and τ2 are assigned to the recombination process in the core states and the surface states, respectively [82]. The τ1 of 1-CQDs takes a large proportion when excited at 360 nm, show that the PAHs clusters origin core state are dominant for blue emission; this value decreases by 6.7 % when excited at 400 nm. Combined with the previous analysis, the hydrothermal carbonization of the ellagitannin is responsible for blue-green emission. The τ2 of 2-CQDs is up to 88.86 %; therefore, its fluorescence decay kinetics is mainly caused by its surface states. It also means that the nitrogen-doping process produces new PL centers. Among these small molecular fluorophores, the flavonoid compound is likely to account for the green emission of 2-CQDs.

The influence of pH and concentration on the PL performance of the prepared CQDs is further discussed, as shown in Fig. 8 . Fig. 8a displays the PL emission behavior of the two CQDs samples in the pH range of 3–11 under the condition of their respective optimal excitation wavelength (360 nm and 420 nm). The PL properties of two CQDs samples display pH dependence. The pH corresponding to the strongest emission peak of them appears at about 4, revealing that the two types of CQDs have good adaptability in acidic aqueous solution. In addition, when the pH value increases from 3 to 11, 1-CQDs emits blue light and weak blue-green light at 446 nm and 495 nm, respectively; however, 2-CQDs displays a green emission center with symmetry at 495 nm. This phenomenon is consistent with the PL emission behavior of the CQDs shown in Fig. 6. Fig. 8b displays the influence of dilution multiple on the PL emission behavior of the CQDs, and the PL emission peak corresponding to the dilution multiple is shown in Fig. 8c. When the dilution multiple increases from 5 to 10, the PL intensity of the two CQDs samples increases and shows a blue shift. The high concentration of CQDs induces the accumulation of CQDs, which leads to a decrease in the intensity of emitted light [83], therefore, it is imperative to control the concentration and overcome self-quenching [84]. When the dilution multiple increases from 10 to 160, the emission peak of 1-CQDs remain unchanged, while the emission peak of 2-CQDs shifts towards the short-wavelength directly (from the green region to the blue region), and the intensity of emission peaks gradually decreases. The results show that the concentration of some molecular fluorophores greatly influences the green emission of 2-CQDs. As discussed in the preceding part of this section, flavonoid compounds are considered as the primary PL centers for the excellent green emission of 2-CQDs. Based on these results, we believe that the complexity and variety of interaction between carbon cores and organic fluorophores result in their different physicochemical properties with each other.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis transmittance performance of the PVA/CS/CQDs films
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9 (a-d) and Table S2 (see ESI) display the effect of the load of CQDs and CS on the UV–vis transmittance performance of the fabricated PVA/CS/CQDs films. It can be seen from Fig. 9(a) and (b) that the introduction of CQDs can significantly reduce the UV transmittance of PVA film, that is, adding the prepared CQDs to the PVA matrix can remarkably improve the UV-blocking ability of PVA. All PVA/CS/CQDs composite films can effectively block UV light by the PL emission of the CQDs, especially in the UV-B region. And within a certain range of the CQDs load, with the increase of CQDs, the UV-blocking ability of PVA composite films is enhanced. However, there seems to be a threshold for CQDs usage. For example, for 1-CQDs composite films, when the amount of CQDs in the PVA composite film exceeds 12 mL, the UV-blocking ability of the composite film decreases instead. Similarly, the threshold for 2-CQDs is 10 mL. It may relate to the high concentration agglomeration of CQDs in the composite film [43]. In addition, the absorbance curves of the CS/CQDs solutions, CS solution and CQDs solutions are shown in Fig. S1 (see ESI), CQDs/CS solutions display higher absorbance than without containing CS solution in the UV region. And, as shown in Fig. 9(c) and (d), the PVA/CS/CQDs films have a stronger UV-blocking ability than that without CS (1CS-0 and 2CS-0), which indicates that the addition of CS into PVA/CQDs matrix can also significantly improve the UV-blocking capability of CQDs. It may be attributed to providing a suitable pH for CQDs by CS (from alkaline to the weak acid condition) and the CS’s structure [35]. Besides, with the increase of CS, the transmittance of the composite film in the visible region decreases significantly, which should be related to the incomplete transparency of CS itself. Fig. 9(e-f) present their specific UV–vis transmittance properties by direct comparison. Compared with 1CQDs-12, 2CQDs-10 has a higher UV-A blocking (94.53 % compared with 83.58 %), a similar UV-B blocking (98.95 % compared with 97.28 %), and a lower transmittance in visible light (visible transmittance,66.06 % compared to more than 79.34 %). Therefore, the 2CQDs-10 displays better UV-blocking performance, while the 1CQDs-12 has a visual advantage in the visible region. It can be concluded from the above discussion that 1CQDs-12 and 2CQDs-10 in Table 1 are the optimal formulations for preparing functionalized PVA with UV-blocking property. Furthermore, the two samples also exhibit appropriate transmittance in visible light.

Considering that the stability of the UV-blocking film is very important for its future application, we have also done experiments on the photobleaching property of the prepared CQDs-based composite film. The UV–vis transmission of 1CQDs-12 and 2CQDs-10 after 6, 12, 24 h of UV irradiation (365 nm) is shown in Fig. 9(g-h). The UV-blocking and transparency of both CQDs-based composite films only changed slightly in the visible region with the increase of the UV irradiation time, indicating that they have good UV-blocking stability. Table 2
[43,85–87] lists the comparison of UV-blocking properties of our PVA-based films, commercially available UV-blocking film and those in other researches. The UV-blocking property of film is evaluated by such indicators as
T UV-A
,
T UV-B
,
T Visible
and UV-blocking factor. Commercial PET-based UV-blocking film has higher UV-blocking factor than CS/2-CQDs (29.2 vs 18.87). However, in order to maintain its high visual transparency, the commercial PET film sacrifices its optical resistance to light at wavelengths of 350–400 nm. Therefore, it is not reliable to protect the film from UV-A (315–400 nm) damage to the chemical bonds in the polymer, resulting in greater photodegradation risks [2]. CS/2-CQDs film has a good resistance to almost the entire UV wavelength range (280–400 nm), but its visual transparency is not as good as the commercial PET-based film. Therefore, the relationship between visual transparency and UV-blocking ability needs to be balanced according to the requirements of the application environment. The prepared films in this study (1CQDs-12 and 2CQDs-10) have a better ability to block UV light and keep a higher transmittance in the visible region, so they are expected to become a new member of the PVA-based UV-blocking materials family. As shown in Fig. 10 (a-b), both 1CQDs-12 and 2CQDs-10 are highly transparent, indicating that CS/CQDs has a good dispersion without apparent aggregation in the PVA matrix. 2CQDs-10 is darker than 1CQDs-12 because it contains flavonoid derivatives. In addition, both 1CQDs-12 and 2CQDs-10 show strong solid-state emission characteristics. When excited by UV light (365 nm), they emit uniform blue and green light, respectively. Fig. 10c shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of 1CQDs-12 film, 2CQDs-10 film and pure PVA film. The pure PVA film presents the lowest absorbance in the UV–vis region. In contrast, the PVA with CS/CQDs shows a sharp and intense absorption peak in the UV region, indicating that CS/CQDs has great potential as an UV absorbent by combining with PVA.

PL emission spectra of 1CQDs-12 and 2CQDs-10 are further investigated, as shown in Fig. 10(d-e). With the increase of excitation wavelength (the excitation wavelength range is 300–420 nm and 350–470 nm for 1CQDs-12 and 2CQD-10, respectively), and the PL intensity of the composite film increases gradually. This experimental phenomenon is inconsistent with that of the CQDs shown in Fig. 6, which confirms that CS has a significant synergistic enhancement on the fluorescence emission of CQDs. In addition, the PL intensity of 1CQDs-12 at about 550 nm is almost the same regardless of the excitation wavelength and 2CQDs-10 has similar behavior at about 575 nm. The difference is that the former has a strong blue PL emission at around 475 nm, while the latter has a strong green PL emission at around 500 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploration of the interaction between CS/CQDs with PVA
ce:section.ce:para: XRD characterization results of CS, CS/1-CQDs, CS/2-CQDs, PVA, PVA/CS/1-CQDs, and PVA/CS/2-CQDs nanocomposite films are shown in Fig. S2 (see ESI). Peak at 20.6° in the diffractogram of CS film is also present in all the CS/CQDs confirming the retention of crystallinity after incorporation of CQDs. Similarly, the pure PVA and its composite film keep at 19.76°. The almost undifferentiated location of this peak in composite films suggested that the addition of CS/CQDs would not disturb the crystallization of PVA. FT-IR spectrum analysis of the composite film is used to discuss the interaction among PVA, CS, and CQDs, as shown in Fig. 11 a. The peak of OH stretching vibration of pure PVA is 3284 cm−1, but when CS/CQDs is added into the PVA matrix, this peak moves to 3260 cm−1, which may be related to the association of intramolecular hydrogen bonds of PVA and the dissociation of new hydrogen bonds among PVA, CS and CQDs [88]. These new hydrogen bonds may be formed when positively charged NH3 + groups in CS or CQDs electrostatically interactions with predominant OH groups in CQDs or PVA [89]. Further evidence comes from zeta potential values, which showed that while chitosan with NH3 + groups are positively charged (+30.58 mV), CQDs prepared from ACs is negatively charged (1-CQDs is −28.53 mV and 2-CQDs is −10.57 mV).The abundant surface chains of PVA cover the CS/CQDs, which prevents the graphitizing cores from π − π interactions. It is also conducive to resist the aggregation-caused-quenching of fluorescence of CQDs [90]. The interaction schematic diagram among PVA, CS and CQDs is shown in Fig. 11(b) and (c). The strong hydrogen-bond interaction among PVA, CS and CQDs is beneficial to the dispersion of CQDs, thus improving the UV absorption ability of the composite films [91]. Furthermore, their SEM micrograph images suggest no obvious microphase separation on the cross-sections and the interface is well bonded, and the CS/CQDs promoting a denser and compact structure for the PVA matrix. The four characterization results prove that the physical interaction (hydrogen bonding interactions) among PVA, CS and CQDs.

The purpose of this study is to explore a clean and high value-added comprehensive utilization approach for ACs. In the hydrothermal carbonization process of ACs, lignocellulose is first decomposed into small fragment clusters, and then self-assembled into carbon networks through condensation or polymerization, and finally grow into carbon spheres with different sizes. Small fluorophore molecules with different optical properties were distributed on surface of carbon spheres. 1-CQDs and 2-CQDs with sp2-hybridized carbon nanodomains and plenty of functional groups were successfully prepared. The average particle size of 2-CQDs (5.23 nm) was higher than that of the 1-CQDs (4.16 nm). The active surface groups of 1-CQDs include Ph-OH, OH, and COOH, etc. And, the nitrogen-doped process introduces new active functional groups into 2-CQDs, such as CNH2, CNC, CNH, and CONH. For 1-CQDs, the carbon core mainly absorbs UV light and converts it into blue emission, while the secondary blue-green emission comes from the ellagitannin. 2-CQDs has strong green emission, mainly generated from hydrothermal carbonization and nitrogen-doped flavonoid compounds of ACs. In addition, CS can be combined with CQDs by hydrogen bonding to enhance the UV absorption capacity of CQDs. The UV-A blocking rates of PVA/CS/1-CQDs (4:6:12) and PVA/CS/2-CQDs (4:8:10) films were 83.58 % and 94.53 %, respectively. Moreover, they also have a high transmittance in visible light. The two CQDs described here and their determined practical application provided an attractive solution to functionalize PVA material and broadened the clean and comprehensive application of biomass resource like ACs.
